result.markdown:
--- title: "Lab 9: Simple Logistic Regression" --- ::: {.objectives} ## What You'll Learn in This Lab - Use logistic regression for a binary outcome - Convert among probability, odds, and log-odds - Fit a logistic regression with `glm(..., family = binomial)` - Read coefficients on the log-odds scale - Report odds ratios with `model_params(..., exponentiate = TRUE)` - Convert fitted values back to predicted probabilities - Distinguish odds-ratio interpretations from probability interpretations - Plot predicted probabilities with `ggeffects::predict_response()` - **Lecture Notes Reference:** Chapter 9: *Simple Logistic Regression* ::: {.meta} Dataset: `titanic`. Estimated time: 75-90 minutes. ::: ::: ## Part 1: Prepare the Data We use the Titanic passenger data from the lecture notes. The outcome is `survived`, coded 1 if the passenger survived and 0 otherwise. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} data(titanic) titanic_lab <- titanic |> mutate( sex_f = factor(sex, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Male", "Female")), survived_f = factor(survived, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Died", "Survived")), first_class = if_else(pclass == 1, 1L, 0L) ) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} tibble( Rows = nrow(titanic_lab), Survival_rate = mean(titanic_lab$survived), Female_rate = mean(titanic_lab$sex), Mean_age = mean(titanic_lab$age, na.rm = TRUE), Missing_age = sum(is.na(titanic_lab$age)) ) |> round_numeric(digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| Rows| Survival_rate| Female_rate| Mean_age| Missing_age| |----:|-------------:|-----------:|--------:|-----------:| | 1309| 0.382| 0.356| 29.881| 263|
::: ::: ::: {.pitfall} Logistic regression expects a binary outcome. In this lab, `survived = 1` is the event we model. Every coefficient describes how a predictor changes the log-odds, odds, or probability of survival. ::: ## Part 2: Start with a Cross-Tabulation Before fitting a model, compute the observed survival rate by sex. Because `survived` is coded 0/1, its mean is the survival probability. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} survival_by_sex <- titanic_lab |> group_by(sex_f) |> summarise( n = n(), n_survived = sum(survived), n_died = n - n_survived, probability = n_survived / n, odds = probability / (1 - probability), log_odds = qlogis(probability), .groups = "drop" ) stopifnot(all(is.finite(survival_by_sex$probability))) stopifnot(all(survival_by_sex$probability >= 0, survival_by_sex$probability <= 1)) survival_by_sex |> round_numeric(digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|sex_f | n| n_survived| n_died| probability| odds| log_odds| |:------|---:|----------:|------:|-----------:|-----:|--------:| |Male | 843| 161| 682| 0.191| 0.236| -1.444| |Female | 466| 339| 127| 0.727| 2.669| 0.982|
::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(survival_by_sex, aes(x = sex_f, y = probability, fill = sex_f)) + geom_col(width = 0.65) + geom_text( aes(label = scales::percent(probability, accuracy = 0.1)), vjust = -0.4, fontface = "bold" ) + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(), limits = c(0, 1)) + scale_fill_manual(values = c( "Male" = ber640_colors[["primary"]], "Female" = ber640_colors[["secondary"]] )) + labs( x = "Passenger Sex", y = "Observed Survival Probability" ) + guides(fill = "none") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Titanic survival probability by passenger sex.](lab09-logistic-intro_files/figure-html/lab09-survival-bar-1.png){fig-alt='Bar chart showing the observed probability of survival for male and female Titanic passengers. Female passengers have a much higher survival probability than male passengers.' width=2100} ::: ::: ::: {.interpretation} The table shows the same information on three scales. The probability scale is intuitive. The odds scale is what odds ratios compare. The log-odds scale is where logistic regression is linear. ::: ## Part 3: Convert Between Scales These three functions are the main conversion tools: - Probability to odds: `p / (1 - p)` - Probability to log-odds: `qlogis(p)` - Log-odds to probability: `plogis(log_odds)` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} scale_examples <- tibble( probability = c(.10, .25, .50, .75, .90) ) |> mutate( odds = probability / (1 - probability), log_odds = qlogis(probability), probability_check = plogis(log_odds) ) scale_examples |> round_numeric(digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| probability| odds| log_odds| probability_check| |-----------:|-----:|--------:|-----------------:| | 0.10| 0.111| -2.197| 0.10| | 0.25| 0.333| -1.099| 0.25| | 0.50| 1.000| 0.000| 0.50| | 0.75| 3.000| 1.099| 0.75| | 0.90| 9.000| 2.197| 0.90|
::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} scale_plot_data <- tibble(probability = seq(.01, .99, by = .01)) |> mutate( Probability = probability, Odds = probability / (1 - probability), `Log-odds` = qlogis(probability) ) |> pivot_longer(-probability, names_to = "Scale", values_to = "Value") ggplot(scale_plot_data, aes(x = probability, y = Value)) + geom_line(color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], linewidth = 1.1) + facet_wrap(~ Scale, scales = "free_y") + labs( x = "Probability", y = "Scale Value" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Probability, odds, and log-odds scales.](lab09-logistic-intro_files/figure-html/lab09-three-scales-plot-1.png){fig-alt='Three-panel line plot showing how probability maps to probability, odds, and log-odds. Probability is bounded between zero and one, odds are bounded below by zero, and log-odds are unbounded.' width=3000} ::: ::: ::: {.pitfall} Odds and probabilities are not the same. An odds ratio of 2 does not usually mean "twice as likely." That shortcut is only close when the event is rare. ::: ## Part 4: Fit a Logistic Regression Model Use `glm()` with `family = binomial`. The default link is the logit link, so the coefficients are log-odds coefficients. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model_sex <- glm( survived ~ sex_f, data = titanic_lab, family = binomial ) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} log_odds_table <- model_params(model_sex) |> as_tibble() |> mutate(p = format_p(p)) log_odds_table |> round_numeric(digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|Parameter | Coefficient| SE| CI| CI_low| CI_high| z| df_error|p | |:-----------|-----------:|-----:|----:|------:|-------:|-------:|--------:|:------| |(Intercept) | -1.444| 0.088| 0.95| -1.619| -1.275| -16.476| Inf|< .001 | |sex_fFemale | 2.425| 0.136| 0.95| 2.162| 2.695| 17.832| Inf|< .001 |
::: ::: ::: {.interpretation} The intercept is the log-odds of survival for the reference group: male passengers. The `sex_fFemale` coefficient is the difference in log-odds between female and male passengers. Most applied reports convert this difference to an odds ratio. ::: ## Part 5: Report Odds Ratios Set `exponentiate = TRUE` to convert log-odds coefficients into odds ratios. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} odds_ratio_table <- model_params(model_sex, exponentiate = TRUE) |> as_tibble() |> rename(Odds_ratio = Coefficient) |> mutate(p = format_p(p)) odds_ratio_table |> round_numeric(digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|Parameter | Odds_ratio| SE| CI| CI_low| CI_high| z| df_error|p | |:-----------|----------:|-----:|----:|------:|-------:|-------:|--------:|:------| |(Intercept) | 0.236| 0.021| 0.95| 0.198| 0.279| -16.476| Inf|< .001 | |sex_fFemale | 11.307| 1.538| 0.95| 8.687| 14.809| 17.832| Inf|< .001 |
::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} manual_or <- survival_by_sex |> summarise(odds_ratio = odds[sex_f == "Female"] / odds[sex_f == "Male"]) model_or <- odds_ratio_table |> filter(Parameter == "sex_fFemale") |> transmute(odds_ratio = Odds_ratio) tibble( Source = c("Manual cross-tab", "Logistic model"), Odds_ratio = c(manual_or$odds_ratio, model_or$odds_ratio) ) |> round_numeric(digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|Source | Odds_ratio| |:----------------|----------:| |Manual cross-tab | 11.307| |Logistic model | 11.307|
::: ::: ::: {.interpretation} With one binary predictor, the logistic regression odds ratio matches the cross-tab odds ratio. The model is doing the same comparison, but it expresses it in the regression framework that we can extend to more predictors later. ::: ::: {.your-turn} [Your turn]{.yt-title} [Write]{.badge-r} Use the odds-ratio table to write one sentence interpreting `sex_fFemale`. Use this structure: > Female passengers had about ___ times the odds of survival compared with male passengers. Then add one sentence explaining what the odds ratio does not mean. ::: ## Part 6: Predicted Probabilities for Groups Odds ratios are compact, but predicted probabilities are easier to communicate. `ggeffects::predict_response()` returns predicted probabilities on the response scale. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sex_predictions <- ggeffects::predict_response(model_sex, terms = "sex_f") |> as_tibble() |> transmute( sex_f = x, predicted_probability = predicted, conf.low, conf.high ) sex_predictions |> round_numeric(digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|sex_f | predicted_probability| conf.low| conf.high| |:------|---------------------:|--------:|---------:| |Male | 0.191| 0.166| 0.219| |Female | 0.727| 0.685| 0.766|
::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sex_probability_contrast <- sex_predictions |> select(sex_f, predicted_probability) |> pivot_wider(names_from = sex_f, values_from = predicted_probability) |> mutate(probability_difference = Female - Male) sex_probability_contrast |> round_numeric(digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| Male| Female| probability_difference| |-----:|------:|----------------------:| | 0.191| 0.727| 0.536|
::: ::: ::: {.your-turn} [Your turn]{.yt-title} [Write]{.badge-r} Write two sentences on the probability scale. 1. State the predicted probability of survival for male passengers and female passengers. 2. State the difference in predicted probabilities using percentage points. ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(sex_predictions, aes(x = sex_f, y = predicted_probability)) + geom_point(size = 3, color = ber640_colors[["primary"]]) + geom_errorbar( aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), width = 0.12, color = ber640_colors[["primary"]] ) + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(), limits = c(0, 1)) + labs( x = "Passenger Sex", y = "Predicted Survival Probability" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Predicted probability of Titanic survival by passenger sex.](lab09-logistic-intro_files/figure-html/lab09-predicted-sex-plot-1.png){fig-alt='Point-and-interval plot showing predicted survival probabilities for male and female Titanic passengers. Female passengers have a substantially higher predicted probability of survival.' width=2100} ::: ::: ::: {.interpretation} The odds ratio and predicted probabilities answer related but different questions. The odds ratio compares survival on the odds scale. The predicted probabilities describe survival on the probability scale. A large odds ratio can correspond to a smaller probability difference because probabilities are bounded between 0 and 1. ::: ::: {.your-turn} [Your turn]{.yt-title} [Write]{.badge-r} Explain why these two statements can both be true: - Female passengers had much higher odds of survival than male passengers. - The predicted probabilities are still numbers between 0 and 1. Use the words **odds**, **probability**, and **bounded**. ::: ## Part 7: A Continuous Predictor Logistic regression also works with continuous predictors. Here we fit a simple model using passenger age. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} titanic_age <- titanic_lab |> drop_na(age) model_age <- glm( survived ~ age, data = titanic_age, family = binomial ) model_params(model_age, exponentiate = TRUE) |> as_tibble() |> mutate(p = format_p(p)) |> round_numeric(digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
|Parameter | Coefficient| SE| CI| CI_low| CI_high| z| df_error|p | |:-----------|-----------:|-----:|----:|------:|-------:|------:|--------:|:-----| |(Intercept) | 0.872| 0.126| 0.95| 0.657| 1.159| -0.943| Inf|0.345 | |age | 0.992| 0.004| 0.95| 0.984| 1.001| -1.792| Inf|0.073 |
::: ::: ::: {.interpretation} For a continuous predictor, the odds ratio is the multiplicative change in odds for a one-unit increase. For `age`, that means the change in odds of survival for each additional year of age. If a one-year unit feels too small, rescale the predictor before fitting the model. ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} age_predictions <- ggeffects::predict_response(model_age, terms = "age [all]") |> as_tibble() ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(titanic_age, aes(x = age, y = survived)) + geom_jitter(height = 0.035, width = 0, alpha = 0.18, color = "grey45") + geom_ribbon( data = age_predictions, aes(x = x, ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), inherit.aes = FALSE, fill = ber640_colors[["accent"]], alpha = 0.18 ) + geom_line( data = age_predictions, aes(x = x, y = predicted), inherit.aes = FALSE, color = ber640_colors[["primary"]], linewidth = 1.1 ) + scale_y_continuous( labels = scales::percent_format(), limits = c(-0.05, 1.05), breaks = seq(0, 1, by = .25) ) + labs( x = "Passenger Age", y = "Survival" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Predicted probability of Titanic survival by passenger age.](lab09-logistic-intro_files/figure-html/lab09-age-probability-plot-1.png){fig-alt='Scatter plot of Titanic survival status by passenger age with a fitted logistic regression curve. The predicted survival probability slopes downward from roughly the mid-40 percent range for children to about one-third by age 80, with the confidence band widest at older ages.' width=2400} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} age_predictions_selected <- ggeffects::predict_response( model_age, terms = "age [10, 30, 50, 70]" ) |> as_tibble() |> transmute( age = x, predicted_probability = predicted, conf.low, conf.high ) age_predictions_selected |> round_numeric(digits = 3) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| age| predicted_probability| conf.low| conf.high| |---:|---------------------:|--------:|---------:| | 10| 0.446| 0.395| 0.498| | 30| 0.408| 0.378| 0.438| | 50| 0.370| 0.322| 0.422| | 70| 0.334| 0.257| 0.421|
::: ::: ::: {.your-turn} [Your turn]{.yt-title} [Write]{.badge-r} Choose two ages from the table. Write one sentence comparing their predicted probabilities of survival. Use probability language, not classification language. ::: ::: {.pitfall} Logistic regression predicts probabilities, not classifications. The curve gives `P(survived = 1)`. Turning those probabilities into "survived" or "died" requires a separate decision threshold, which belongs to diagnostics and classification. ::: ::: {.exercises} ## What to Submit Submit the following items: 1. The odds-ratio table for `model_sex`. [core]{.chip .easy} 2. One sentence interpreting the `sex_fFemale` odds ratio using odds language. [core]{.chip .med} 3. The predicted-probability table for `sex_f`. [core]{.chip .easy} 4. Two sentences interpreting the predicted probabilities and probability difference. [core]{.chip .med} 5. One short explanation of why an odds ratio and a probability difference are not the same quantity. [core]{.chip .med} 6. The selected-age predicted-probability table and one sentence comparing two ages. [core]{.chip .med} ::: ::: {.summary} ## Lab Summary 1. Logistic regression is for binary outcomes. 2. `glm(..., family = binomial)` estimates coefficients on the log-odds scale. 3. `exp(coef)` or `model_params(..., exponentiate = TRUE)` converts log-odds coefficients to odds ratios. 4. Probability, odds, and log-odds are different scales for the same event probability. 5. `plogis()` converts log-odds to probabilities. 6. `ggeffects::predict_response()` gives simple predicted probability tables and plots. **Next:** Lab 10 extends logistic regression to multiple predictors and adjusted predictions. :::
